concerns.text: T014 enforces a retention threshold of '>= 95% of original cohort' after excluding high-motion subjects. This contradicts Spec FR-001 and Edge Case logic which mandate excluding subjects with FD >0.5mm regardless of the resulting cohort size. The task effectively forces the retention of bad data or fails the pipeline if the 'clean' cohort drops below 95%, violating the spec's explicit exclusion criteria.

T033 cites '(Gershon et al., n.d.)' for the NIH Toolbox validation. This violates Constitution Principle II (Verified Accuracy) which requires every external citation to be verified against a primary source before contributing. A placeholder 'n.d.' cannot be verified by the Reference-Validator Agent, creating a blocking gate violation.

The sensitivity analysis logic is fragmented and risks violating FR-009/SC-003. T019 generates graphs for all densities, but T020/T021 calculate metrics without explicitly iterating over all densities. T031 then re-runs the 'full graph and stats pipeline' for robustness checks. This creates ambiguity: are metrics computed once for all densities (efficient) or redundantly (wasteful)? The task list fails to clearly define the single coherent data structure required by the spec for sensitivity analysis.

T014 filters subjects with FD >0.5mm but lacks a verification step to ensure the *final retained dataset* meets the Spec FR-002 quality metric of 'mean framewise displacement <= 0.2 mm'. The task only excludes outliers; it does not verify the aggregate quality of the remaining cohort, leaving the success criterion of FR-002 unmeasured.

T030 describes adaptive sampling but is vague on the specific fallback logic required by FR-007. FR-007 mandates reducing to the 'maximum count achievable within the remaining time' with a hard floor of 'minimum 1,000 permutations'. T030's description of 'reduce permutation count' risks an implementation that drops below the 1,000 minimum or fails to calculate the 'remaining time' buffer correctly, violating the spec's hard constraints.
